article.title: Curiosity's First TMAH Wet Chemistry Run on Mars Yields More Than 20 Organic Molecules, Seven Never Detected Before on the Planet
article.summary: A Nature Communications paper published April 21 reports that the first off-Earth thermochemolysis experiment, performed on a clay-rich mudstone in Gale crater, expanded Mars's known organic inventory and detected nitrogen heterocycles for the first time on the surface.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Curiosity rover has produced the most diverse inventory of organic molecules ever measured on Mars, after scientists ran the rover's onboard wet-chemistry experiment for the first time on a sample drilled from the floor of Gale crater. According to a [NASA announcement](https://www.nasa.gov/missions/mars-science-laboratory/curiosity-rover/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/) on April 21, 2026, the analysis identified 21 carbon-containing molecules in a single sample, seven of which had never been confirmed on the Martian surface before.

The results were published the same day in Nature Communications by a team led by Amy Williams, a geological sciences professor at the University of Florida, and represent the first use of tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAH) thermochemolysis on another planet, [JPL reported](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/).

## What We Know

The sample, nicknamed "Mary Anning 3," was drilled in October 2020 from a clay-rich mudstone in the Glen Torridon region of Gale crater, an area that ancient lakes and streams once filled, [according to NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/missions/mars-science-laboratory/curiosity-rover/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/). Curiosity's Sample Analysis at Mars (SAM) instrument suite carried only two cups of TMAH solvent for the entire mission, and Mary Anning 3 was the first sample exposed to one of them, [JPL reported](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/).

Unlike SAM's standard pyrolysis runs, which heat samples in a dry oven, the TMAH experiment performs "wet chemistry" by dropping the powdered rock into a solvent that hydrolyzes and methylates complex macromolecular structures, breaking them into smaller fragments that can be vaporized and identified by gas chromatography and mass spectrometry, [JPL reported](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/). The technique is designed to liberate organic carbon that earlier dry-heating methods could not free.

Among the molecules detected for the first time on the Martian surface are benzothiophene, methyl benzoate, naphthalene, methylnaphthalene, dihydronaphthalene, trimethylbenzene, and tetramethylbenzene, [Chemistry World reported](https://www.chemistryworld.com/news/more-than-20-organic-compounds-found-on-mars-many-for-the-first-time/4023329.article). The same outlet notes that the team also detected a possible nitrogen-containing aromatic compound, identified as a candidate dimethylindole, which would be the first nitrogen heterocycle confirmed on Mars.

"Nitrogen heterocycles have never been found before on the Martian surface or confirmed in Martian meteorites," Williams said, [according to NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/missions/mars-science-laboratory/curiosity-rover/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/). The class of molecules includes the nucleobases that make up DNA and RNA on Earth.

The SAM instrument is a miniaturized chemistry lab housed inside Curiosity, [JPL described](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/). It receives drilled rock powder, heats it to release gases, and routes those gases through a gas chromatograph and a quadrupole mass spectrometer to identify what is present.

## What the Molecules Imply

The new organic catalog matters less for any single compound than for what their preservation implies. Williams told [Chemistry World](https://www.chemistryworld.com/news/more-than-20-organic-compounds-found-on-mars-many-for-the-first-time/4023329.article) that "the story our findings tell is that there is complex organic carbon preserved in the very near subsurface of Mars, where we thought radiation would have destroyed all of that." The Glen Torridon mudstones are roughly 3.5 billion years old, and ionizing radiation at the Martian surface has long been considered a major obstacle to the survival of large organic molecules over those timescales.

The team is careful to separate the chemistry from any claim about life. Williams told [Chemistry World](https://www.chemistryworld.com/news/more-than-20-organic-compounds-found-on-mars-many-for-the-first-time/4023329.article) that while her group has found building blocks of life, she cannot say whether they derive from past biology, geological processes, or organic material delivered by meteorites. Janice Bishop of the SETI Institute, who was not part of the study, told the same outlet that "detection of a variety of complex organics on Mars is really exciting because it implies the presence of molecules that could be important for prebiotic chemistry or life."

Project scientist Ashwin Vasavada framed the result in habitability terms, saying the new collection of organics "once again increases the prospect that Mars offered a home for life," [according to NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/missions/mars-science-laboratory/curiosity-rover/nasas-curiosity-finds-organic-molecules-never-seen-before-on-mars/).

## What We Don't Know

The central uncertainty is provenance. As [CNN reported](https://edition.cnn.com/2026/04/24/science/curiosity-rover-organic-molecules-mars), the compounds could in principle have formed through abiotic geochemistry on Mars, arrived on the planet via meteoritic delivery, or been produced by ancient biology, and the SAM data alone cannot distinguish among those origins.

A second open question concerns the larger molecules that TMAH was designed to break apart. The detected fragments suggest the original rock contains macromolecular organic matter, but the team does not yet have a structural picture of what that parent material looks like, [Chemistry World noted](https://www.chemistryworld.com/news/more-than-20-organic-compounds-found-on-mars-many-for-the-first-time/4023329.article).

Resolving these questions will likely require returning Martian samples to laboratories on Earth, where instruments far more sensitive than anything that can fit inside a rover can be used. NASA's Mars Sample Return campaign, which would retrieve samples cached by the Perseverance rover in Jezero crater, remains in early development.

## Looking Forward

With one of Curiosity's two TMAH cups now used, the rover has only one wet-chemistry shot remaining. [Engadget reported](https://www.engadget.com/science/space/nasas-curiosity-rover-found-promising-organic-chemicals-on-mars-174514375.html) that the data from this first run will inform when and where to deploy the second, and that the methodology will help shape similar experiments on the European Space Agency's Rosalind Franklin rover and on NASA's Dragonfly mission to Titan.

For a rover that landed in 2012 and has spent the past decade climbing Mount Sharp, the result is a reminder that legacy missions can still produce first-of-their-kind science when their full instrument suite is finally exercised.
